title: New York Fed Releases Report on the Political Origins of Section 13(3) of the Federal Reserve Act

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The New York Fed released a press release announcing a research article by Parinitha Sastry examining the political and legislative history of Section 13(3) of the Federal Reserve Act, which was added in 1932 during the Great Depression. The article explores Congress's intent in granting the Federal Reserve expanded emergency lending authority to include individuals, partnerships, and corporations. The author concludes that the original framers intended to enable the Fed to lend directly to the real economy during emergencies.
key_points: Section 13(3) was added in 1932 at the height of the Great Depression
The provision expanded Fed lending authority to include individuals, partnerships, and corporations in emergencies
The article is published in the Economic Policy Review, a policy-oriented journal focused on macroeconomic, banking, and financial market topics
Authored by Parinitha Sastry, former senior research analyst at the New York Fed
The analysis focuses on Congress's rationale for endowing the central bank with emergency lending authority
The research provides insights into the evolving role of the Federal Reserve as an emergency liquidity provider
why_it_matters: Section 13(3) is the statutory authority the Federal Reserve used during the 2008 financial crisis to establish emergency lending facilities (such as TALF and Maiden Lane transactions). Understanding the original legislative intent behind this authority provides context for current debates about the Fed's emergency lending powers and their appropriate scope.
market_structure_context: While Section 13(3) has significant market structure implications as the authority enabling Fed emergency lending to nonbank institutions during crises, this source is a press release announcing a historical legislative analysis rather than a discussion of current market structure or operations. It does not address the Fed's current balance sheet, operating framework, or emergency lending programs. The source contains no substantive research findings—only an announcement summary.
